transcript.text: 275 and 285. Also in stereo on VHF. This is National Radio 1.
Well, hello there. TV on Radio. Thomas the Vance here. And welcome once again to the Friday Rock Show.
Tonight, three archive sets by Stevie Winwood, Jim Capaldi, Dave Mason and Chris Wood. The four musicians who got it together in Aston Tirrold in Berkshire.
And became the band called Traffic. Also, we have the latest set by Public Image Limited.
We've got classic cuts by Queen, Deep Purple, a real classic by Jimmy Page, something by Focus and various others as well.
New releases from John and Van Gelis and also two tracks from the forthcoming album by Rush.
A new single by Simple Minds and we're going to replay that eighth single by Trespass.
But tonight we start with Jefferson, Starship and Jane.
And that, as if you didn't know, is from the album Queen Live Killers.
It was Queen and Sheer Heart Attack, and I played it, and splayed it as well, and sprayed it,
saying I'm speaking into a microphone for the good friends of Philip Littleford,
of number 118 Hopton Road, Upper Cam, near Dursley in Gloucestershire.
And the good friends of Steve R and Paul and Ash and Mildew, I think it is, strange name,
Nick and Bev and Sarah F, Sarah H and Mandy, who are going to be listening after coming home from a youth club.
Hope you had a good time tonight at the youth club.
Also for Stephen Freer and all his mates up there in Edinburgh.
And prior to that, you heard Jefferson Starship,
a single that they've released from their album,
a good album, too.
The album's called Freedom at Point Zero.
The single is called Jane.
A couple of weeks ago, or maybe it was just last week,
we played a track, a single by a band called Trespass.
And the single is called One of These Days.

asking where they can get this single.
Stay tuned, and I will give you the address of Trial Records.

is something new from an album yet to come out by Rush.

and the track is Spirit of the Radio.

21 minutes before 11 o'clock on the Friday Rock Show
Wednesday the 9th was the birthday of Mr. Jimmy Page.
Now, we thought, to sort of celebrate it,
we'd play something very, very different by Mr. Page.
This was issued as a single in February 1965,
but deleted from the Fontana catalogue in August 1965,
so it's a rarity.
I believe, in fact, if you can find it as a single,
it's worth something like 30 quid.

and also to sort of indicate his delight at the way that Jimmy Page

The British Rock and Pop Awards.

Best Male Singer, Best Female Singer, Best Group or Band, Best Single and Best LP.
Remember, all your nominations must be chosen from last year, 1979.
